worth_it_explanation: Samba TV produces a single deduplicated cross-screen reach figure that counts each household once, regardless of how many screens it was exposed on. Reading the per-screen figures and adding them together produces a higher number, but that number counts households reached on more than one screen multiple times. These checks confirm that the deduplicated figure is the one used for planning and reporting, that frequency is read from the deduplicated cross-screen data rather than from a single screen, and that practitioners understand the matching methodology well enough to reconcile Samba reach against per-platform delivery logs. Each check is a reporting and methodology review inside the Samba TV platform, not a technical reconfiguration.
intro: Samba TV measures reach and frequency across linear TV, connected TV (CTV), and digital using Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data from opt-in smart TVs. When ACR data confirms a household was exposed to a campaign on more than one screen, Samba counts that household once in its cross-screen reach total rather than once per screen. The deduplicated cross-screen reach figure is the primary output of this measurement. These checks confirm that practitioners use the deduplicated figure as the basis for reach and frequency analysis, understand what the household matching methodology produces, and can reconcile the Samba reach number against per-platform delivery reports without drawing incorrect conclusions.

audit_checks.title: Per-screen reach figures are summed rather than using the deduplicated cross-screen reach total
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A campaign that reached 2 million households on linear TV and 1.5 million households on CTV did not necessarily reach 3.5 million unique households. Any household exposed on both screens appears in both per-screen figures. Summing the two overstates total reach, and the size of the overstatement grows with the share of cross-screen households in the audience. Reporting an inflated reach number to a client or internal stakeholder produces incorrect conclusions about campaign efficiency and cost per unique household.

educate.base:
Samba TV tracks ad exposure using ACR data collected from opt-in smart TVs. When a household watches a linear TV broadcast that carries a campaign ad, ACR records that exposure. When the same household streams on a CTV device or is matched to a digital impression, Samba records those exposures as well. Because Samba can link these exposures to the same household, it produces a single deduplicated cross-screen reach figure that counts the household once regardless of how many screens it appeared on.

The per-screen reach columns in a Samba TV report show how many households were reached on each screen individually. A household that saw the ad on both linear TV and CTV appears in both the linear reach column and the CTV reach column. Adding those two columns together produces a number larger than the true unique household reach. The difference between the sum of per-screen figures and the deduplicated cross-screen figure is the count of households that were reached on more than one screen. In a campaign with substantial linear and CTV overlap, this difference can represent a meaningful share of the total.

The deduplicated cross-screen reach figure, sometimes labeled 'Total Unduplicated Reach' or 'Cross-Screen Reach' in Samba reports, is the correct figure to use when reporting how many unique households a campaign reached. Per-screen reach figures are appropriate for understanding which individual screens contributed to the campaign, but they should not be summed to produce a total reach claim.

A few signs this check applies:
  • A post-campaign summary reports total reach as the sum of the linear TV reach and the CTV reach columns from the Samba report
  • The reporting template does not include a cross-screen or unduplicated reach row, and the analyst has no record of where the total reach number came from
  • The reported reach figure is higher than the deduplicated cross-screen figure in the Samba report, with no explanation for the difference
You may want to confirm which field in the Samba report was used as the total reach number before reporting campaign results to a client or leadership.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail brand running a campaign across linear TV and streaming, the cross-screen deduplicated reach figure tells you how many households were exposed to the campaign at least once, regardless of screen. You may want to confirm that cost-per-household calculations use this figure rather than the sum of per-screen reach columns, so the efficiency metric reflects actual unique household coverage.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using Samba TV to measure household-level reach across screens, you may want to confirm the total reach figure in the campaign report corresponds to the cross-screen deduplicated reach row in Samba, not a sum of linear and CTV reach figures. Overstated reach understates cost per unique household reached.

educate.learn_more_note: Samba TV's cross-screen measurement methodology documentation, including the definition of deduplicated reach and the matching approach, is available to clients and agency partners through the Samba TV platform and account team. Contact your Samba TV account representative for access.

investigate.intro: Open the Samba TV campaign report and locate the cross-screen reach and per-screen reach figures to confirm which value was used in post-campaign reporting.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV reporting interface and navigate to the campaign in question.
Locate the reach summary section. Identify the cross-screen or unduplicated reach row, which counts each household once regardless of how many screens it appeared on.
Identify the per-screen reach rows for linear TV, CTV, and any digital placements included in the report.
Add the per-screen figures together and compare the sum to the deduplicated cross-screen reach figure. Note the difference in absolute terms and as a percentage of the deduplicated total.
Compare the total reach figure reported in any campaign summary document, slide deck, or client report against the deduplicated cross-screen reach figure in Samba. Confirm they match.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the deduplicated cross-screen reach figure from the Samba report, the sum of per-screen reach figures, and the total reach figure used in any campaign reporting document. If the reported total reach matches the deduplicated cross-screen figure, this check passes. If the reported total reach matches or approximates the sum of per-screen figures and is higher than the deduplicated figure, this check is a fail, recorded with the deduplicated figure, the summed figure, and the difference.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the deduplicated cross-screen reach, the sum of per-screen figures, and the reach total used in campaign reporting above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Samba TV campaign reporting uses the sum of per-screen reach figures rather than the deduplicated cross-screen reach total. Households exposed on more than one screen appear in each per-screen reach column, so the sum overstates how many unique households the campaign reached. {{voice_recommend}} identifying the cross-screen deduplicated reach figure in the Samba report and using that as the basis for total reach and cost-per-household calculations in all campaign summaries.

audit_checks.title: Frequency is read from a single screen rather than from deduplicated cross-screen exposure data
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A household reached twice on linear TV and once on CTV has an actual frequency of three exposures. Reading frequency only from the linear delivery report shows two. The frequency figure drives decisions about creative rotation, budget pacing, and whether a campaign is overexposing a core audience, so an understated frequency number can allow overexposure to go undetected.

educate.base:
Frequency in a multi-screen campaign is the average number of times a household was exposed to the campaign across all screens, counted together. Samba TV calculates this figure using its cross-screen matched data, so a household exposed twice on linear TV and once on CTV contributes three exposures to the average, not two.

When frequency is read from a single screen, such as only from the linear TV delivery report or only from the CTV impression log, the result is an average exposure count for that screen alone. That figure does not reflect how many times the household actually saw the campaign. If linear TV and CTV carry similar creative and target overlapping audiences, the true cross-screen frequency can be meaningfully higher than either per-screen figure suggests.

Underestimated frequency has two practical consequences. First, a campaign that has already reached its intended frequency threshold on a per-household basis may appear to have room for additional delivery when per-screen frequency looks low. This can lead to continued spend against households that are already oversaturated. Second, post-campaign attribution analysis that estimates purchase lift or recall from exposure needs accurate frequency data to model the relationship between exposure count and outcome. Frequency figures drawn from a single screen do not give the model the full exposure picture.

A few signs this check applies:
  • Post-campaign analysis references average frequency drawn from the linear TV buy or from a single platform's delivery report rather than from the Samba cross-screen report
  • The Samba report includes a cross-screen frequency column but it was not included in the summary sent to the client or decision-maker
  • Frequency caps are set independently on linear and CTV without any household-level view of combined exposure
You may want to confirm that the frequency figure cited in campaign reporting comes from Samba's cross-screen data rather than from a single platform's delivery log.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running the same campaign creative on both linear TV and streaming, a household that sees the ad on both screens has a higher actual frequency than either screen's log shows individually. You may want to confirm the frequency figure in the post-campaign analysis uses Samba's cross-screen exposure data so creative rotation and re-flight decisions reflect the actual household-level exposure count.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser trying to reach a target household a set number of times before a promotional period ends, you may want to confirm that the frequency metric used to track progress toward that goal comes from the Samba cross-screen report rather than from any single-channel delivery log.

educate.learn_more_note: Samba TV's documentation on cross-screen frequency calculation, including how ACR (Automatic Content Recognition) data is used to attribute exposures at the household level, is available through the Samba TV platform and account team. Contact your Samba TV account representative for access.

investigate.intro: Locate the frequency figure cited in any campaign summary and confirm whether it was drawn from the Samba cross-screen report or from a single platform's delivery log.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV campaign report and locate the cross-screen frequency column. This shows the average number of exposures per unique household across all measured screens.
Note the per-screen average frequency rows if they appear in the report. Compare each to the cross-screen frequency.
Review the post-campaign summary document or slide deck. Identify which frequency figure was cited and trace it back to its source in the Samba report.
If the cited frequency matches a per-screen figure rather than the cross-screen figure, note the difference between the two.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the cross-screen average frequency from the Samba report and the frequency figure cited in any campaign summary or client-facing document. If the cited figure matches the cross-screen frequency, this check passes. If the cited figure matches a per-screen frequency and is lower than the cross-screen frequency, this check is a fail, recorded with both figures and the source of the cited number.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the cross-screen frequency from the Samba report and the frequency figure cited in campaign reporting above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that frequency figures in {{site_name}}'s Samba TV campaign reporting are drawn from a single screen rather than from the cross-screen exposure data. Households reached on more than one screen accumulate exposures across each, so per-screen frequency understates how many times a household actually saw the campaign. {{voice_recommend}} using the cross-screen average frequency from the Samba report as the basis for all frequency-related decisions, including creative rotation thresholds, pacing adjustments, and post-campaign attribution analysis.

audit_checks.title: The household matching basis behind Samba's deduplication is not understood when reconciling reach against per-platform delivery logs
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Samba TV's cross-screen reach figure will differ from a linear TV buy's gross rating point (GRP) report and from a CTV platform's impression log. Without understanding why the figures differ, practitioners may conclude that one source is wrong and use the other as the authoritative number. If the wrong source is chosen, reach and efficiency calculations are built on an inaccurate foundation.

educate.base:
Samba TV derives its cross-screen reach figures from ACR data collected on opt-in smart TVs. When a Samba-enabled TV in a household is on and receiving a broadcast signal or streaming content, the ACR sensor reads the screen and matches what it sees against Samba's content and ad catalog. This gives Samba a household-level record of linear TV exposure without relying on the broadcaster's impression reporting. For CTV and digital exposure, Samba ingests impression data from ad servers and matches it to its household graph.

A linear TV buy is measured by the network or broadcaster using set-top box data, panel data, or a combination. That measurement counts audiences differently from Samba's ACR-based approach. Differences in how each system defines an impression, how it handles co-viewing in a household, and how it handles panel or ACR coverage gaps mean the reach figures will not match. This is expected. A 15 to 30 percent difference between Samba reach and a network's reported GRP-based reach is within a range practitioners typically accept as a methodological difference, not an error.

Problems arise when a practitioner encounters a discrepancy and draws the wrong conclusion: that one source must be measuring incorrectly and should be dismissed. The more useful interpretation is to understand what each source measures and what produces the gap. Samba's ACR panel covers households with Samba-enabled smart TVs in its measurement footprint. The network's measurement covers all households that received the broadcast. The two populations overlap but are not identical, and neither is a complete census. Knowing this allows the practitioner to explain the discrepancy to a client rather than having to choose a side in a dispute between two valid but different measurement approaches.

A few signs this check applies:
  • A campaign report shows a meaningful difference between Samba's cross-screen reach and the reach reported by the linear TV network or the CTV platform, and no explanation accompanies the discrepancy
  • The practitioner has dismissed the Samba figure because it differs from the platform report, or vice versa, without documenting the methodological reason for the difference
  • A client or internal stakeholder has raised a question about which reach number is correct, and there is no prepared answer based on how each source measures
You may want to review the basis for Samba's reach measurement and the basis for the per-platform delivery figure before presenting both in the same report, so the discrepancy can be explained rather than left unresolved.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running a campaign across a linear TV buy and a CTV platform with Samba TV measurement running alongside both, you may want to confirm that the team understands why the Samba cross-screen reach figure differs from each platform's individual delivery report before the post-campaign debrief. A documented reconciliation note prevents the client from concluding that one of the measurement sources is unreliable.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using Samba TV to track household-level reach across a multi-platform buy, you may want to confirm that any comparison of Samba reach against platform delivery data includes a note on the matching methodology so differences are understood as expected variance rather than errors in reporting.

educate.learn_more_note: Samba TV's documentation on its ACR panel methodology, household matching approach, and guidance for reconciling cross-screen reach against per-platform delivery data is available to clients and agency partners through the Samba TV platform and account team. Contact your Samba TV account representative for access.

investigate.intro: Review the campaign's post-campaign or in-flight report for any discrepancy between the Samba cross-screen reach figure and reach or impression figures from the linear buy or CTV platform, and confirm whether a reconciliation explanation was documented.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV campaign report and note the cross-screen deduplicated reach figure for the campaign.
Retrieve the linear TV network's reach or GRP report for the same campaign and date range. Note the household reach or audience delivery figure.
Retrieve the CTV platform's delivery report and note its impression and reach figure.

Calculate the percentage difference between the Samba cross-screen reach and each per-platform figure. Note whether a reconciliation explanation or methodology note was documented alongside these figures.
Ask the team whether the Samba account team provided a measurement guide or reconciliation note explaining the expected relationship between Samba reach and the network or CTV platform figures.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the Samba cross-screen reach figure, the reach or delivery figure from the linear buy or CTV platform, the percentage difference between the two, and whether a reconciliation explanation or methodology note was documented. If a reconciliation note exists and explains the difference as expected methodological variance, this check passes. If the discrepancy is present but unexplained, or if one figure was dismissed without documentation, this check is a fail, recorded with both figures and the percentage difference.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Samba cross-screen reach, the per-platform reach or delivery figure, the percentage difference, and whether a reconciliation explanation exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Samba TV reports show a discrepancy between the cross-screen reach figure and the reach or delivery figures from the linear TV buy or CTV platform, with no reconciliation explanation on file. Samba's ACR-based household measurement and the per-platform delivery reports use different methodologies and different population footprints, so differences are expected and do not indicate an error in either source. {{voice_recommend}} documenting a reconciliation note for each campaign that explains the expected variance between Samba cross-screen reach and per-platform delivery data, so discrepancies can be presented to clients or stakeholders as methodological differences rather than unresolved conflicts between measurement sources.
